cards.q: Why is blood transfusion especially important in tropical obstetrics?
cards.a: Because haemorrhage is the number one cause of maternal mortality and morbidity.

cards.q: What principle governs the use of blood transfusion in O&G?
cards.a: It should be used only to save the mother's life, given HIV/AIDS risk and difficulty recruiting voluntary donors.

cards.q: What is the criticism often made of transfusion in maternal deaths?
cards.a: That it was 'too little, too late'.

cards.q: Where should women at high risk of losing >1000 ml deliver?
cards.a: In a setting where blood transfusion and intensive care facilities are available.

cards.q: What is the major risk of blood transfusion?
cards.a: The patient receiving an incorrect blood component.

cards.q: What is component therapy?
cards.a: Separating a unit of whole blood into components (red cells plus platelets, cryoprecipitate or FFP) and giving only the specific component needed.

cards.q: Why is component therapy the standard method?
cards.a: It provides better targeted treatment and conserves blood, since components from one unit can treat several patients; it has made whole blood rarely necessary.

cards.q: List the indications for transfusion in O&G.
cards.a: Symptomatic anaemia including heart failure; Hb less than 5.6 g/dl; anaemia very close to term; anaemia in labour; and acute haemorrhage/operative loss >30% of blood volume or >500 ml not maintained by plasma expanders.

cards.q: At what Hb level is transfusion indicated?
cards.a: A very low Hb of less than 5.6 g/dl.

cards.q: What is the indication for red blood cells?
cards.a: To increase oxygen-carrying capacity in anaemic women and for orthostatic hypotension from blood loss.

cards.q: What is the indication for platelet concentrates?
cards.a: To control or prevent bleeding associated with deficiencies in platelet number or function.

cards.q: What is the indication for fresh frozen plasma?
cards.a: To increase the level of clotting factors in patients with demonstrated deficiency.

cards.q: What does cryoprecipitate contain and when is it used?
cards.a: Factors I, V, VIII, XIII, von Willebrand factor and fibronectin; used for deficiency of fibrinogen, factor VIII, factor XIII, fibronectin or vWF.

cards.q: List the types of transfusion.
cards.a: Whole blood, packed cells (sedimented blood), blood component therapy, exchange blood transfusion and autologous blood transfusion.

cards.q: What is the only indication for exchange blood transfusion in anaemia of pregnancy?
cards.a: When correction of anaemia must be made very quickly — e.g. severe anaemia in early labour.

cards.q: What is autologous transfusion?
cards.a: The collection and reinfusion of the patient's own blood.

cards.q: Name the three autologous techniques.
cards.a: Preoperative autologous blood donation, intraoperative blood (cell) salvage, and acute normovolaemic haemodilution.

cards.q: What are the requirements for preoperative autologous donation?
cards.a: Donation at least 2 weeks before surgery/delivery, with an adequate Hb of 11 g/dl or more.

cards.q: Is pre-delivery autologous donation routinely recommended?
cards.a: No — few parturients require transfusion, so routine donation is not justified; but it may be appropriate for placenta praevia.

cards.q: What is the rate of vasovagal reactions in autologous donors?
cards.a: About 2-5%.

cards.q: What is intraoperative cell salvage (IOCS)?
cards.a: The sterile collection, washing and reinfusion of shed blood, using a continuous-flow centrifugation device such as a Cell Saver.

cards.q: What are the contraindications to intraoperative cell salvage?
cards.a: Infection, contamination with malignant cells, and penetrating bowel wounds.

cards.q: How long can salvaged blood be stored, and can it be given to others?
cards.a: At room temperature up to 4 hours (or ≤24 h in the blood bank if properly identified); it must never be transfused to any other patient.

cards.q: What is acute normovolaemic haemodilution?
cards.a: Removal of blood before/after induction of anaesthesia with simultaneous crystalloid to maintain normovolaemia, and reinfusion of the patient's RBCs at the end of surgery.

cards.q: What are the benefits of acute normovolaemic haemodilution?
cards.a: Fewer RBCs are lost during surgery (lower Hb concentration), fresh whole blood is available, and lower blood viscosity improves tissue perfusion and oxygenation.

cards.q: When should blood group and antibody status be checked in pregnancy?
cards.a: At booking and at 28 weeks gestation.

cards.q: What compatibility is required for red cell transfusion?
cards.a: ABO, RhD and K (Kell) compatibility.

cards.q: What blood is issued in a major obstetric haemorrhage before the group is known?
cards.a: Group O RhD-negative, K-negative units, switching to group-specific blood as soon as feasible.

cards.q: What type of blood should be used for elective transfusion in pregnancy and intrauterine transfusion?
cards.a: CMV-seronegative blood (and components).

cards.q: Why is correct identification of each unit critical?
cards.a: Because the major risk is giving the wrong blood; it often requires a second individual to cross-check before transfusion.

cards.q: When is warming of blood needed before transfusion?
cards.a: Rarely — only when the rate exceeds 50 ml/kg/hr (risk of transfusion-induced hypothermia) or in cold agglutinin disease.

cards.q: What is the purpose of a leucocyte filter?
cards.a: To prevent febrile non-haemolytic transfusion reactions and lower the risk of CMV infection (but it removes most platelets if used for platelet transfusion).

cards.q: Which fluid may be infused through the same line as blood, and why only that one?
cards.a: Only normal saline (0.9%); other crystalloids cause agglutination/haemolysis, and calcium-containing solutions cause clotting in the tubing.

cards.q: Why should medications not be added to a unit of blood?
cards.a: Interactions are unpredictable, reactions become hard to attribute, and stopping the transfusion means an incomplete drug dose.

cards.q: What are the major complications of blood transfusion?
cards.a: Infections, alloimmunisation and transfusion reactions.

cards.q: What causes acute haemolytic transfusion reactions and what are the features?
cards.a: Usually ABO incompatibility causing intravascular haemolysis, with chills, fever, chest and flank pain, nausea, cardiovascular collapse and DIC.

cards.q: What causes febrile non-haemolytic reactions and how are they managed?
cards.a: Recipient antibodies against leucocyte and platelet antigens; managed with antipyretics, and leucocyte-reduced components if recurrent.

cards.q: Which blood groups cause delayed haemolytic reactions and what are the features?
cards.a: Kidd (Jk), Duffy (Fy) and Kell; usually extravascular, with fever, anaemia, hyperbilirubinaemia and a positive direct Coombs test, occurring 7-10 days after transfusion.

cards.q: Why should CMV-negative blood be used in the undelivered parturient?
cards.a: Because of the fetal immune status — to avoid CMV transmission via intrauterine or maternal transfusion.

cards.q: What is Patient Blood Management (PBM)?
cards.a: An all-encompassing approach to avoid unnecessary transfusion: optimise pre-delivery Hb, avoid over-transfusion, use cell salvage, accept lower transfusion triggers, and use IV/oral iron in stable non-bleeding women.

cards.q: Outline the management of a pregnant Jehovah's Witness refusing transfusion.
cards.a: Multidisciplinary antenatal planning to minimise anaemia and manage blood loss; early iron replacement including IV iron; discuss and document consent/refusal antenatally; use pharmacological, mechanical and surgical measures early to avoid banked blood; and fully inform her of the risks sensitively, without coercion.

cards.q: CLINICAL — In major PPH with unknown group, what red cells do you give first?
cards.a: Group O RhD-negative, K (Kell)-negative red cells, switching to group-specific as soon as feasible.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient develops chills, fever, flank pain and collapse minutes into a transfusion. What is the reaction and cause?
cards.a: An acute haemolytic reaction from ABO incompatibility (intravascular haemolysis) — stop the transfusion immediately.

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient develops jaundice, anaemia and a positive Coombs test 8 days after transfusion. What is this?
cards.a: A delayed haemolytic reaction, typically from Kidd, Duffy or Kell antibodies (extravascular haemolysis).

cards.q: CLINICAL — A patient develops an isolated fever during transfusion with no haemolysis. Reaction and management?
cards.a: Febrile non-haemolytic reaction; give antipyretics and use leucocyte-reduced components if it recurs.

cards.q: CLINICAL — Which obstetric condition makes preoperative autologous donation appropriate?
cards.a: Placenta praevia (a known high bleeding risk).

cards.q: CLINICAL — Why can't you run Ringer's lactate with blood?
cards.a: It contains calcium, which causes clotting in the tubing; only normal saline may share the line.
